EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A carnival worker died at the site of the Wilson County Fair.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 48-year-old worker, helping to set up the Wilson County Fair, died while working outdoors. He rested inside his vehicle, but he did not turn on the air conditioner inside his truck. According to Mr. David McMurray, affiliated with Tennessee OSHA, he sat inside his truck with the doors open. The worker was from New Jersey. He became nauseous and fatigued while working, so he decided to rest. Coworkers could not revive him when he was found unconscious around 4 PM CST Wednesday August 15. Temperatures at Nashville International Airport reached 104 degrees with the dew point temperature in the middle 50s. This was below the heat advisory criteria of 105.   |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions started in March 2007, but its effects were being felt in May by farmers. The quality of hay was poorer, and yield was down by as much as 60 to 70 percent. There were 14 consecutive days in May, specifically from May 17-30, when no rain fell in Nashville. There was a trace of rain on May 31. May had 3.30 inches of rain which was 1.77 inches below normal.   ||The longest period on record for no rain was 36 consecutive days from September 11, 1923 through October 16, 1923.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near to below normal rainfall during the month of July, continued the severe drought(D2) conditions over portions of West Tennessee, while other locations near the Tennessee River, the drought remained extreme drought(D3).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe drought(D2)continued over portions of West Tennessee during July. For areas near the Tennessee River, the drought remained extreme(D3).  Rainfall was near normal to about 3 inches below normal in July which brought the yearly deficit to around 15 to 18 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low level pressure system moved into the Lower Mississippi Valley during the afternoon and early evening hours of July 6th, 2007. The combination of the upper low and warm, moist air produced scattered showers and thunderstorms. Some of the thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall that caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell 3 miles southwest of Arlington and produced flash flooding. Water covered Highway 70 in several locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved into Arkansas during the afternoon and evening hours of July 10th, 2007. The trough produced scattered showers and thunderstorms ahead of the trough over West Tennessee. Some of the thunderstorms produced dangerous lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the Middlecoff Dental Group building. The strike caused significant damage to office equipment inside the building. No injuries occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through West Tennessee during the early afternoon hours of June 24, 2007. The disturbance interacted with deep moisture producing showers and thunderstorms over the area. Some of the thunderstorms became severe producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down some trees on Highway 77/70 Alternate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms developed over West Tennessee on June 26, 2007 due to daytime heating. Some of the thunderstorms became severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked a few trees down on Highway 194, 3 miles north of Oakland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms developed over West Tennessee on June 26, 2007 due to daytime heating. Some of the thunderstorms became severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
Lightning struck a house in the Stoneybrook subdivision.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the roof of a house in the Stoneybrook subdivision, located at 106 Hogan Lane. Firefighters responded within 15 minutes, but the fire had already spread quickly through the house. Firefighters spent 6 hours fighting the fire and securing the area. The fire fully-engulfed the house, and a 1964 Lincoln Continental in the driveway with 102,000 miles. There were no injuries in this blaze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning strikes a utility building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a utility building on RT Davis Rd. It caught fire, and the building and its contents were completely destroyed. The building housed antiques and flammable materials that were used to refurbish the antiques.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought was easing up in the western part of Middle Tennessee in November. The City of Portland in Sumner County lifted water restrictions along with the City of Lafayette. ||The April spring freeze and drought was hard on Christmas tree growers. The Volunteer Tree Farm in Lebanon lost about 5,000 trees.  ||The Heritage Academy, which is a Christian boarding school in Monterey, had to limit its water use. The campus has 50 staff members and 44 high school students which depend on 2 wells for water. The school was planning to dig for another well.||Governor Phil Bredesen told communities that the state would help those who are running out of water, but repairs to their infrastructures are their responsibility. The state can help with engineering plans and with applications for grants from the Appalachian Regional Commission.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought continues through November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought was easing up in the western part of Middle Tennessee in November. The City of Portland in Sumner County lifted water restrictions along with the City of Lafayette. ||The April spring freeze and drought was hard on Christmas tree growers. The Volunteer Tree Farm in Lebanon lost about 5,000 trees.  ||The Heritage Academy, which is a Christian boarding school in Monterey, had to limit its water use. The campus has 50 staff members and 44 high school students which depend on 2 wells for water. The school was planning to dig for another well.||Governor Phil Bredesen told communities that the state would help those who are running out of water, but repairs to their infrastructures are their responsibility. The state can help with engineering plans and with applications for grants from the Appalachian Regional Commission.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought continues through November.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newspaper article reported roof damage in the downtown area. There was damage to a chain link fence. Also, a stop sign was blown down. The roof was blown off a building housing Attorney Gary Howell and the State Probation Office on North Main St. The roof blew across the street and hit two parked cars. Then, the roof crashed through Jim Bailey's vacant drug store. There were several trees down on Main St. Power lines were down at one house. Trees were down on Mt. Zion Road. A tree fell near a house on Mt. Joy Road and damaged some electrical lines. |There was minor roof damage to a school in Mt. Pleasant. In Columbia, the severe thunderstorm took off a section of the red tin roof covering the second and third grade hall at Woodward Elementary School. No one was in class at the time because of spring break.

As this cold front continued to push further southeast during the day on the 25th, thunderstorms ahead of the front produced isolated large hail as well damaging winds during the afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms fired up ahead of a strong cold front on April 3, 2007. There were numerous reports of hail and wind damage. One tornado occurred in Cumberland County at 0916 PM CDT. Snow flurries were reported on Good Friday, April 6, and record cold morning low temperatures were common on Easter Sunday morning with readings in the teens and 20s across Middle Tennessee. Nashville Airport had a record low of 23 degrees Easter Sunday morning. A late season hard freeze hit the mid state. Lows temperatures across Middle Tennessee Friday morning were: 18-29 degrees, Saturday morning lows were 13-27 degrees, and Sunday morning lows were from 17-27 degrees. There were numerous damage to the  berries, fruit trees, and wheat crops across Middle Tennessee amounting to about $100,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Dispatch reported a large tree limb was blown down.

A strong cold front pushed southeast into southern middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours. Very moist air and limited instability ahead of the front resulted in severe thunderstorms and one tornado in Lincoln County Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm wind downed trees down on residential property 5 miles south southeast of Fayetteville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed southeast into southern middle Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours. Very moist air and limited instability ahead of the front resulted in severe thunderstorms and one tornado in Lincoln County Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed ahead of a strong cold front that moved through southern middle Tennessee.  The storm intensified over southeastern Lincoln county between 5:30 and 5:35 PM.  This thunderstorm  produced an EF-1 tornado one mile south southwest of Flintville at 5:35 PM.  This tornado exhibited maximum wind speeds of 90 mph and a path width of 75 yards.  A storage shed was destroyed and a pole barn suffered considerable damage.  As the tornado continued east northeast,  a large oak tree, at least 50 years old, was uprooted just northeast of the pole barn. The track continued in an east northeast direction until it lifted two and one half miles east of Flintville. In other locations along the path EF-0 damage was evident.  Tree were blown down and tops of trees snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast from Texas into Southern Mississippi during the afternoon and evening hours of 1/31/07. Cold air was already in place over West Tennessee thus precipitation associated with the system fell as snow. Light accumulations occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across Obion County and produced accumulations of 1 to 2 inches. Many roadways were covered as a result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning is blamed as the cause of a fire that damaged two condos by fire and water, and two other condos with smoke damage. The fire was in the Renegade Mountain region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked east along the Gulf Coast. The tight pressure gradient between the low pressure system and high pressure located over the Central Plains produced strong winds over West Tennessee on the evening of 1/4/07.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and power lines were knocked down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather broke out ahead of a cold front pushing into West and Middle Tennessee on the afternoon and evening of Nov. 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to a home from the tornado. The house, located on Green Road, loss its front porch. A few trees were uprooted. One tree was snapped at its trunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked through the western and northern Gulf of Mexico and brought a flow of moisture northward into the Tennessee Valley. This resulted in a wintry mix of precipitation including snow, sleet and freezing rain beginning by late evening on the 1st, and lasting through the early morning hours of the 2nd. Warmer air drawn northward changed the wintry precipitation to rainfall or drizzle by mid to late morning. Snow amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches across far northern Alabama and southern middle Tennessee, with some sleet accumulation mixed in. Freezing rain produced only minor ice accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow up to 2 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved through the mid south on its way into Southwest Virginia and Eastern Tennessee.  A large bow developed on the line late in the evening as it approached the Cumberland Plateau. Damage was predominantly created by straight line winds. However, an EF1 Tornado also developed on the Cumberland Plateau in Claiborne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down across the county although the greatest concentration of tree damaged was in the vicinity of Lake City.  Also Lighthouse Marina on Norris Dam sustained damage to the docks and boats.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary across the region produced scattered supercell thunderstorms across the Cumberland Plateau.  Damage was predominately caused by straight-line winds; however, an EF1 tornado tracked across Claiborne County for the second time this month.  Its path was within one mile of the EF1 tornado path that occurred three weeks ago.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down on Fords Chapel Road between New Hope Road and Barren Creek Road.  A few trees were snapped in this area.  The tornado tracked northeast along Barren Creek Road where multiple mobile homes were damaged, one blown over and destroyed.  Tree damage was extensive along Barren Creek Road, with large trees both snapped and uprooted.  Some homes were also damaged, with a patio roof blown back and shingles and siding blown off. The tornado tracked parallel to Highway 33, snapping trees and causing minor damage to homes in subdivisions on Mountain Road, Rutherford Drive, Harmon Road, and Rowe Street.  The tornado crossed Highway 33 near the city limits of New Tazewell before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved through the mid south on its way into Southwest Virginia and Eastern Tennessee.  A large bow developed on the line late in the evening as it approached the Cumberland Plateau. Damage was predominantly created by straight line winds. However, an EF1 Tornado also developed on the Cumberland Plateau in Claiborne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado rated EF1 started five miles northwest of New Tazewell and ended five miles south of Tazewell. The length was approximately 12 miles long and 150 yards wide. The greatest damage occurred across New Tazewell where several trailers were blown off their foundations. Also, trees were blown down county-wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms during the early morning hours of March 28, 2007|produced numerous lightning strikes over portions of West Tennessee. One lightning strike caused a structure fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a chemical fire to start at the Helena Chemical Plant in Humboldt. The chemical fire destroyed the plant. About 400 to 500 employees of nearby plants as well as some 100 nearby residents were evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm that developed in northern Alabama along an outflow boundary in the afternoon moved northeast into southern middle Tennesse and dropped some hail on portions of Franklin County, TN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail occurred at the 911 center in Winchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across the Mid Mississippi Valley during the afternoon hours and moved southeast into the Tennessee Valley during the overnight hours. After several thunderstorms mergered with the line as it moved into southern middle Tennessee, the squall line intensified and accelerated southeast across northern Alabama. A tornado developed near the merger of the squall line with a thunderstorm that developed ahead of the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail was reported as a line of thunderstorms moved southeast across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved through the mid south on its way into Southwest Virginia and Eastern Tennessee.  A large bow developed on the line late in the evening as it approached the Cumberland Plateau. Damage was predominantly created by straight line winds. However, an EF1 Tornado also developed on the Cumberland Plateau in Claiborne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down at various locations throughout the county.  A garage and a small wooden barn were also damaged near Greeneville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall during the month of April brought severe drought conditions to portions of Southwest Tennessee by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe drought(D2), which began in mid April over portions of Southwest Tennessee and continued through the end of the month. Rainfall was about 3 inches below normal which brought the yearly deficit to around 9 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms fired up ahead of a strong cold front on April 3, 2007. There were numerous reports of hail and wind damage. One tornado occurred in Cumberland County at 0916 PM CDT. Snow flurries were reported on Good Friday, April 6, and record cold morning low temperatures were common on Easter Sunday morning with readings in the teens and 20s across Middle Tennessee. Nashville Airport had a record low of 23 degrees Easter Sunday morning. A late season hard freeze hit the mid state. Lows temperatures across Middle Tennessee Friday morning were: 18-29 degrees, Saturday morning lows were 13-27 degrees, and Sunday morning lows were from 17-27 degrees. There were numerous damage to the  berries, fruit trees, and wheat crops across Middle Tennessee amounting to about $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions started in March 2007, but its effects were being felt in May by farmers. The quality of hay was poorer, and yield was down by as much as 60 to 70 percent. May had 3.30 inches of rain which was 1.77 inches below normal.   ||The longest period on record for no rain was 36 consecutive days from September 11, 1923 through October 16, 1923.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved over much of West Tennessee during the early morning hours of April 8, 2007. Record-breaking low temperatures occurred over the area as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record-breaking low temperatures occurred the morning of April 8, 2007. Lows dropped into the upper teens to lower 20s which caused considerable crop and vegetation damage. Leaves were literally frozen off of trees. Corn that had six to seven leaves or beyond was killed. Winter wheat suffered the most harm. 75 to 80 percent of the winter wheat crop was destroyed. In response to the crop and vegetation damage, the governor of Tennessee declared an agricultural disaster for the entire state of Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved over much of West Tennessee during the early morning hours of April 7, 2007. Record-breaking low temperatures occurred over the area as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record-breaking low temperatures occurred the morning of April 7, 2007. Lows dropped into the mid to upper 20s which caused considerable crop and vegetation damage. Leaves were literally frozen off of trees. Corn that had six to seven leaves or beyond was killed. Winter wheat suffered the most harm. 75 to 80 percent of the winter wheat crop was destroyed. In response to the crop and vegetation damage, the governor of Tennessee declared an agricultural disaster for the entire state of Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into West Tennessee during the late afternoon and evening hours of April 3, 2007. The front produced numerous showers and thunderstorms many of which became severe. Damaging winds and large hail occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping pong size hail fell 3 miles south of Rose Creek to 3 miles north of Ramer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms during the morning and afternoon hours of March 31. 2007 produced dangerous lightning strikes. One in which struck a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a fire at a house on Shea Road. The fire caused substantial damage to the home. Four firefighters were treated for heat exhaustion and two other firefighters were treated for other injuries. The fire took 4 to 5 hours to put out. The lightning bolt apparently traveled across the top of the house. The house was under construction and was a few weeks from being completed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong (for July) upper trough formed over the Eastern Great Lakes and Upper Ohio Valley creating cold temperatures aloft.  A meso-scale convective complex developed in this environment across Kentucky during the morning and advanced southeast into the MRX CWFA by afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down across the north half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tennessee State Department of Agriculture has estimated the crop loss in the 100's of millions of dollars. Death of tissue is still progressing. The heat has increased the death process. Loss of inventory or sales will result in buyers going elsewhere. Tennessee may not recover from all of this for 5 to 8 years. Seed on trees have been killed, and this has limit replanting; dogwoods have lost 3 yrs out of their cycle.  ||Older dogwood trees are stressed but living at the moment. A lack of moisture, with a deficit of 12 inches, coupled with record heat at the moment, has stressed out vegetation. There is little irrigation potential across board. ||The drought was in full swing by Mid-March, and by the end of June, many locations are running at least 10 inches below normal rainfall. All of Middle Tennessee has been classified in a drought with the southern tier of counties classified in a Severe Drought.||Cherry Creek Nursery in Cookeville has seen 40% of trees planted this spring die from lack of water. Cherry Creek Nursery lost one million dollars worth of Japanese maple trees to the late spring hard freeze. A vegetable farmer in Ridgetop, TN has been running pumps to move water to his fields for 16-hour days. This costs $300-500 a week. Some hard hit crops are winter wheat and hay, soybeans and tobacco. The first half of 2007 may be among the top five driest ever recorded in Middle Tennessee. The driest year was in 1941.||Many farmers are buying hay from other farmers at 3 times the normal price. Corn production was down 50 percent in Sumner County. Forage hay is 1/3 of what it should be. The poor hay production has affected the state's livestock sector. Poor pasture conditions and reduced hay production has caused farmers to sell off part of their herds.||State Department of Agricultural Commissioner Ken Givens said hay production is down 50-80 percent, and three-fourths of the state's pasture last week were rated in bad condition. The growth of soybeans and corn has been stunted. ||Winter wheat yield is projected to be one half of last year's record yield, which brought in $42.6 million. ||So far, agricultural losses in Coffee County has reached $10 million since the beginning of the drought. ||Commercial wineries will have to rely on imported grapes. The late spring hard freeze and drought has dried up locally grown grapes. |About 30 commercial wineries produce between $7 and $8 million of wine each year, but the University of Tennessee's plant sciences professor David Lockwood expects an 80% crop loss this year. ||On June 15, all Tennessee Counties were designated primary disaster areas because of losses caused by the drought. This would make qualified farm operators eligible for a low-interest emergency loans.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought impacting crops. See details in episode narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tennessee State Department of Agriculture has estimated the crop loss in the 100's of millions of dollars. Death of tissue is still progressing. The heat has increased the death process. Loss of inventory or sales will result in buyers going elsewhere. Tennessee may not recover from all of this for 5 to 8 years. Seed on trees have been killed, and this has limit replanting; dogwoods have lost 3 yrs out of their cycle.  ||Older dogwood trees are stressed but living at the moment. A lack of moisture, with a deficit of 12 inches, coupled with record heat at the moment, has stressed out vegetation. There is little irrigation potential across board. ||The drought was in full swing by Mid-March, and by the end of June, many locations are running at least 10 inches below normal rainfall. All of Middle Tennessee has been classified in a drought with the southern tier of counties classified in a Severe Drought.||Cherry Creek Nursery in Cookeville has seen 40% of trees planted this spring die from lack of water. Cherry Creek Nursery lost one million dollars worth of Japanese maple trees to the late spring hard freeze. A vegetable farmer in Ridgetop, TN has been running pumps to move water to his fields for 16-hour days. This costs $300-500 a week. Some hard hit crops are winter wheat and hay, soybeans and tobacco. The first half of 2007 may be among the top five driest ever recorded in Middle Tennessee. The driest year was in 1941.||Many farmers are buying hay from other farmers at 3 times the normal price. Corn production was down 50 percent in Sumner County. Forage hay is 1/3 of what it should be. The poor hay production has affected the state's livestock sector. Poor pasture conditions and reduced hay production has caused farmers to sell off part of their herds.||State Department of Agricultural Commissioner Ken Givens said hay production is down 50-80 percent, and three-fourths of the state's pasture last week were rated in bad condition. The growth of soybeans and corn has been stunted. ||Winter wheat yield is projected to be one half of last year's record yield, which brought in $42.6 million. ||So far, agricultural losses in Coffee County has reached $10 million since the beginning of the drought. ||Commercial wineries will have to rely on imported grapes. The late spring hard freeze and drought has dried up locally grown grapes. |About 30 commercial wineries produce between $7 and $8 million of wine each year, but the University of Tennessee's plant sciences professor David Lockwood expects an 80% crop loss this year. ||On June 15, all Tennessee Counties were designated primary disaster areas because of losses caused by the drought. This would make qualified farm operators eligible for a low-interest emergency loans.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought impacting crops. See details in episode narrative.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tennessee State Department of Agriculture has estimated the crop loss in the 100's of millions of dollars. Death of tissue is still progressing. The heat has increased the death process. Loss of inventory or sales will result in buyers going elsewhere. Tennessee may not recover from all of this for 5 to 8 years. A lack of moisture, with a deficit of 12 inches, coupled with record heat at the moment, has stressed out vegetation. There is little irrigation potential across board. ||The drought was in full swing by Mid-March, and by the end of June, many locations are running at least 10 inches below normal rainfall. All of Middle Tennessee has been classified in a drought with the southern tier of counties classified in a Severe Drought.||Cherry Creek Nursery in Cookeville has seen 40% of trees planted this spring die from lack of water. Cherry Creek Nursery lost one million dollars worth of Japanese maple trees to the late spring hard freeze. A vegetable farmer in Ridgetop, TN has been running pumps to move water to his fields for 16-hour days. This costs $300-500 a week. Some hard hit crops are winter wheat and hay, soybeans and tobacco. The first half of 2007 may be among the top five driest ever recorded in Middle Tennessee. The driest year was in 1941.||Many farmers are buying hay from other farmers at 3 times the normal price. Corn production was down 50 percent in Sumner County. Forage hay is 1/3 of what it should be. The poor hay production has affected the state's livestock sector. Poor pasture conditions and reduced hay production has caused farmers to sell off part of their herds.||State Department of Agricultural Commissioner Ken Givens said hay production is down 50-80 percent, and three-fourths of the state's pasture last week were rated in bad condition. The growth of soybeans and corn has been stunted. ||Winter wheat yield is projected to be one half of last year's record yield, which brought in $42.6 million. ||So far, agricultural losses in Coffee County has reached $10 million since the beginning of the drought. ||Commercial wineries will have to rely on imported grapes. The late spring hard freeze and drought has dried up locally grown grapes. |About 30 commercial wineries produce between $7 and $8 million of wine each year, but the University of Tennessee's plant sciences professor David Lockwood expects an 80% crop loss this year. ||On June 15, all Tennessee Counties were designated primary disaster areas because of losses caused by the drought. A squall line moved through the mid south on its way into Southwest Virginia and Eastern Tennessee.  A large bow developed on the line late in the evening as it approached the Cumberland Plateau. Damage was predominantly created by straight line winds. However, an EF1 Tornado also developed on the Cumberland Plateau in Claiborne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down in various locations across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse thunderstorms developed across the forecast area creating some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down in Jasper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse thunderstorms developed across the forecast area creating some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were reported down in southeast Marion county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse thunderstorms developed each afternoon due to strong insolation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees and some power lines were reported down in Jasper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong (for July) upper trough formed over the Eastern Great Lakes and Upper Ohio Valley creating cold temperatures aloft.  A meso-scale convective complex developed in this environment across Kentucky during the morning and advanced southeast into the MRX CWFA by afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms struck early Thursday morning on March 1 and later in the afternoon on the same day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: TDOT reported trees down from the southwest part of the county to the northeast section.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across the Mid Mississippi Valley during the afternoon hours and moved southeast into the Tennessee Valley during the overnight hours. After several thunderstorms mergered with the line as it moved into southern middle Tennessee, the squall line intensified and accelerated southeast across northern Alabama. A tornado developed near the merger of the squall line with a thunderstorm that developed ahead of the line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado formed in extreme southwest Lincoln County, TN between Taft and Ardmore. Some trees were damaged near the Macedonia Church, which is very near the state line.  The tornado moved southeast across the state line into northwestern Madison County, Alabama.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man was struck by lightning June 23, 2007 during an outdoor festival.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was struck by lightning while attending an outdoor festival on June 23, 2007. The 47-year-old Indiana man was among a crowd watching a stage show. A bolt of lightning struck the man, knocking him unconscious. No one else was injured. He was leaning against a motorcycle when the bolt hit his right shoulder and exited out both of his feet according to Sheriff's Deputy Sgt. Durham. He was initially taken to Livingston Regionally Hospital, and then he was transferred to Cookeville Regional Hospital. He died the following day on Sunday June 24. ||The Junebug Boogie festival is held every summer on private property in the Waterloo area of Overton County. It draws motorcycle and music fans from all over the country. It features shows, games, exhibitions, and contests in the open air. The festival ran for four days from June 21 through June 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Governor Phil Bredesen had requested federal disaster drought assistance aid for farmers across all 95 counties in Tennessee on July 5, 2007. The request was approved by the USDA on July 31. The assistance will give an opportunity for farmers to receive low interest loans. ||The impact of the drought continued into July. The Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) average system inflow above Chickamauga Dam for the week ending July 8 was 4,010 cubic feet per second (cfs). That inflow for the week ending July 8 ranks 105th out of 105 years of record for this time of year. Tennessee has 37 drinking water supply systems taking voluntary or mandatory conservation measures. That is up from 36.||The lack of rainfall is making it difficult for TVA to keep river reservoirs full. As a result, hydroelectric power production is being cut by 50 percent. ||TVA is having to purchase electricity from more expensive sources, namely from electric plants that produce electricity from fossil fuels.|This increased cost to TVA will be passed along to consumers as early as October 1, 2007.||The Jackson County Utility District issued a State of Emergency and water restrictions rules for its customers. Second time violators of these restrictions will have their water meter taken out until restrictions are lifted and fines are paid.||The State of Emergency was declared by the Red Boiling Springs Mayor and Water Commissioner. The State of Emergency was issued on July 17th.

Below normal rainfall during the month of June, continued and expanded severe drought(D2) conditions, while other locations in West Tennessee, the drought intensified to an extreme drought(D3).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe drought(D2)continued and expanded into much of West Tennessee by the end of June. The drought intensified over portions of Southwest Tennessee by the end of the month and became extreme(D3). Rainfall was about 3 inches below normal in June which brought the yearly deficit to around 15 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced severe weather with strong winds and large hail on August 24th and 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported roof debris along I-24 at exit #114. Report was verified by sheriff dispatcher. Newspaper articles said major roof damage was done to several hotels with about $550,000 worth of damage. The article stated that damage occurred to the roofs of the Days Inn, Ramada Inn, the Super 8 Motel, and Huddle House. 85 percent of the roof was gone from these businesses. Several vehicles were damaged in nearby parking lots. The Days Inn and Ramada Inn hotels were right off the I-24 exit. ||More than 50 people were displaced at the hotels. The displaced people were moved to nearby hotels. ||Also, a large tree was blown down in Manchester in front of the courthouse. Trees and power lines were also down in other parts of Manchester as well. 2000 people were without power because of the storm.||The article also mentioned that the Quik-Mart located at 2400 Hillsboro Blvd. sustained $2000 worth of damage from aluminum siding that was ripped off the building.

Below normal rainfall during the month of June, continued and expanded severe drought(D2) conditions, while other locations in West Tennessee, the drought intensified to an extreme drought(D3).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe drought(D2)continued and expanded into much of West Tennessee by the end of June. The drought intensified over portions of Southwest Tennessee by the end of the month and became extreme(D3). Rainfall was about 3 inches below normal in June which brought the yearly deficit to around 15 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests.

Tennessee crop losses in 2007 could total $750 million because of the drought. Cotton, tobacco, soybean, and hay yield will be down 38%-48% from last year. Farmers, whose lands make up 45% of the state, are trying to make do. Some sold their cattle early to slaughter, some brought in hay from other regions, and some have payed to buy water to fill up ponds.  ||Water levels at Lake Portland were 12-14 feet below normal. The lake is spring fed and cannot meet the water needs for the City of Portland. City officials are using water from the White House Utility District and Drake's Creek to meet its water needs.||Effects of the drought continues through October in Middle Tennessee.|According to the Tennessee Wildlife Resource Agency (TWRA), area wildlife are not at the point of starvation, even though their food supply is low. Deer are expected to reduce their reproduction next year and have high fawn mortality due to the drought. ||Many area streams have dried up or are running at very low levels because of the drought. Fish have become stressed  by low water flows, high water temperatures and competition for food and space. The stressed conditions have caused spawning failures or increased predation on young fish.||Birds have been affected by the drought. Hummingbirds have been at feeders this summer because of fewer flowers were available. This year's wheat crop yield has been low. This has caused some birds such as morning doves to lose a portion of their food supply. ||It would take several years of a drought to cause a severe effect on area wildlife. ||Tobacco farmers in Macon County will be hit hard. A crop may bring in $15-million in a good year, but in 2007 it may bring in less than $10-million. Perhaps as much as 1000 acres of the county's 4,500 acres of tobacco will not be brought in due to the poor quality of the tobacco. Macon County has been approved to conduct an Emergency Conservation Program (ECP) signup for drought relief. Three practices are available to help with the water shortage. One is for storage facilities, including tanks, troughs, constructing and deepening wells for livestock. Second, is pond cleanout and trying to fill the pond back up. The third phase is hauling water for livestock.||As a result of the Wolf Creek Dam repair and the drought, water levels  at Dale Hollow Lake have resulted in 9 boat launching ramps being not usable.   Lake Cumberland, KY is at 680 feet, which is 43 feet below normal because of the Wolf Creek Dam repair. ||East Fork Utility District Board voted to place mandatory water restrictions into place for its customers in southeast Overton County.     |The City of Monterey provides water to the Utility District. There is|about 140 days of water left.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought continues this month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some normal to above normal rainfall fell across the Central Tennessee Valley during October.  The first fifteen days of the month were essentially rain-free, except for a few spotty light showers that amounted to a tenth of an inch or less where they did occur.||Temperatures, for the most part, stayed well above normal for the first half of the month as well.  ||Periodic rain began during the second half of the month as the storm track moved southward over the area.  The major event of the month was a slow-moving upper low that parked over the area from the 22nd through the 25th.  October rain amounts ranged from one to three inches in Moore, Franklin and Lincoln Counties, the heaviest of which fell in Lincoln County.||The rain was a nice short-term situation but dry conditions quickly reestablished by the end of the month. Exceptional drought (D4) improved slightly to the Extreme (D3) category in northwest portions of Moore and Lincoln Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some normal to above normal rainfall fell across the Central Tennessee Valley during October.  The first fifteen days of the month were essentially rain-free, except for a few spotty light showers that amounted to a tenth of an inch or less where they did occur.||Temperatures, for the most part, stayed well above normal for the first half of the month as well.  ||Periodic rain began during the second half of the month as the storm track moved southward over the area.  The major event of the month was a slow-moving upper low that parked over the area from the 22nd through the 25th.  October rain amounts ranged from one to three inches in Moore, Franklin and Lincoln Counties, the heaviest of which fell in Lincoln County.||The rain was a nice short-term situation but dry conditions quickly reestablished by the end of the month. Exceptional drought (D4) improved slightly to the Extreme (D3) category in northwest portions of Moore and Lincoln Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some normal to above normal rainfall fell across the Central Tennessee Valley during October. The first fifteen days of the month were essentially rain-free, except for a few spotty light showers that amounted to a tenth of an inch or less where they did occur.||Temperatures, for the most part, stayed well above normal for the first half of the month as well.||Periodic rain began during the second half of the month as the storm track moved southward over the area. The major event of the month was a slow-moving upper low that parked over the area from the 22nd through the 25th. October rain amounts ranged from one to three inches in Moore, Franklin and Lincoln Counties, the heaviest of which fell in Lincoln County.||The rain was a nice short-term situation but dry conditions quickly reestablished by the end of the month. Exceptional drought (D4) improved slightly to the Extreme (D3) category in northwest portions of Moore and Lincoln Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tennessee crop losses in 2007 could total $750 million because of the drought. Cotton, tobacco, soybean, and hay yield will be down 38%-48% from last year. Farmers, whose lands make up 45% of the state, are trying to make do. Some sold their cattle early to slaughter, some brought in hay from other regions, and some have payed to buy water to fill up ponds.  ||Water levels at Lake Portland were 12-14 feet below normal. The lake is spring fed and cannot meet the water needs for the City of Portland. City officials are using water from the White House Utility District and Drake's Creek to meet its water needs.||Effects of the drought continues through October in Middle Tennessee.|According to the Tennessee Wildlife Resource Agency (TWRA), area wildlife are not at the point of starvation, even though their food supply is low. Deer are expected to reduce their reproduction next year and have high fawn mortality due to the drought. ||Many area streams have dried up or are running at very low levels because of the drought. Fish have become stressed  by low water flows, high water temperatures and competition for food and space. The stressed conditions have caused spawning failures or increased predation on young fish.||Birds have been affected by the drought. Hummingbirds have been at feeders this summer because of fewer flowers were available. This year's wheat crop yield has been low. This has caused some birds such as morning doves to lose a portion of their food supply. ||It would take several years of a drought to cause a severe effect on area wildlife. ||Tobacco farmers in Macon County will be hit hard. A crop may bring in $15-million in a good year, but in 2007 it may bring in less than $10-million. Perhaps as much as 1000 acres of the county's 4,500 acres of tobacco will not be brought in due to the poor quality of the tobacco. Macon County has been approved to conduct an Emergency Conservation Program (ECP) signup for drought relief. Three practices are available to help with the water shortage. One is for storage facilities, including tanks, troughs, constructing and deepening wells for livestock. Second, is pond cleanout and trying to fill the pond back up. The third phase is hauling water for livestock.||As a result of the Wolf Creek Dam repair and the drought, water levels  at Dale Hollow Lake have resulted in 9 boat launching ramps being not usable.   Lake Cumberland, KY is at 680 feet, which is 43 feet below normal because of the Wolf Creek Dam repair. ||East Fork Utility District Board voted to place mandatory water restrictions into place for its customers in southeast Overton County.     |The City of Monterey provides water to the Utility District. There is|about 140 days of water left.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought continues this month.

Some sold their cattle early to slaughter, some brought in hay from other regions, and some have payed to buy water to fill up ponds.  ||Water levels at Lake Portland were 12-14 feet below normal. The lake is spring fed and cannot meet the water needs for the City of Portland. City officials are using water from the White House Utility District and Drake's Creek to meet its water needs.||Effects of the drought continues through October in Middle Tennessee.|According to the Tennessee Wildlife Resource Agency (TWRA), area wildlife are not at the point of starvation, even though their food supply is low. Deer are expected to reduce their reproduction next year and have high fawn mortality due to the drought. ||Many area streams have dried up or are running at very low levels because of the drought. Fish have become stressed  by low water flows, high water temperatures and competition for food and space. The stressed conditions have caused spawning failures or increased predation on young fish.||Birds have been affected by the drought. Hummingbirds have been at feeders this summer because of fewer flowers were available. This year's wheat crop yield has been low. This has caused some birds such as morning doves to lose a portion of their food supply. ||It would take several years of a drought to cause a severe effect on area wildlife. ||Tobacco farmers in Macon County will be hit hard. A crop may bring in $15-million in a good year, but in 2007 it may bring in less than $10-million. Perhaps as much as 1000 acres of the county's 4,500 acres of tobacco will not be brought in due to the poor quality of the tobacco. Macon County has been approved to conduct an Emergency Conservation Program (ECP) signup for drought relief. Three practices are available to help with the water shortage. One is for storage facilities, including tanks, troughs, constructing and deepening wells for livestock. Second, is pond cleanout and trying to fill the pond back up. The third phase is hauling water for livestock.||As a result of the Wolf Creek Dam repair and the drought, water levels  at Dale Hollow Lake have resulted in 9 boat launching ramps being not usable.   Lake Cumberland, KY is at 680 feet, which is 43 feet below normal because of the Wolf Creek Dam repair. ||East Fork Utility District Board voted to place mandatory water restrictions into place for its customers in southeast Overton County.     |The City of Monterey provides water to the Utility District. There is|about 140 days of water left.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought continues this month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some normal to above normal rainfall fell across the Central Tennessee Valley during October.  The first fifteen days of the month were essentially rain-free, except for a few spotty light showers that amounted to a tenth of an inch or less where they did occur.||Temperatures, for the most part, stayed well above normal for the first half of the month as well.  ||Periodic rain began during the second half of the month as the storm track moved southward over the area.  The major event of the month was a slow-moving upper low that parked over the area from the 22nd through the 25th.  October rain amounts ranged from one to three inches in Moore, Franklin and Lincoln Counties, the heaviest of which fell in Lincoln County.||The rain was a nice short-term situation but dry conditions quickly reestablished by the end of the month. Exceptional drought (D4) improved slightly to the Extreme (D3) category in northwest portions of Moore and Lincoln Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into West Tennessee during the evening hours of October 18th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front. Some of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 foot television antenna was blown over in Dyer. Two large trees were blown down on a house in Trenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into West Tennessee during the evening hours of October 18th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front. Some of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some trees were knocked down on Allison Street in Paris and near Springville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic blast of unseasonably record cold produced an unprecedented hard freeze to the entire Tennessee Valley on the mornings of April 7 and 8, and either freezing or near freezing conditions on the preceding (6th) and following day (9th). This resulted in total losses of area fruit crops including strawberries, blueberries, and peaches.  Pecans, corn and wheat crops were also damaged.  Economic losses totalling in the millions of dollars are anticipated. Lows in many valley locations dropped into the upper 20s both mornings.  Many locations, especially rural, experienced temperatures below 32 degrees Fahrenheit from 20 to 30 hours over the span of the 6th through 10th. |Normally, the probability of temperatures dipping into the 20s this late in the season is 10 percent or less. Also of note, NASA MODIS remote sensing satellite imagery over the central Tennessee Valley revealed distinct changes from green vegetative and tree color before to an auburn and brown color, especially notable atop the Cumberland Plateau region of northeast Alabama and southern middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic blast of unseasonably record cold produced an unprecedented hard freeze to the entire Tennessee Valley on the mornings of April 7 and 8, and either freezing or near freezing conditions on the preceding (6th) and following day (9th). This resulted in total losses of area fruit crops including strawberries, blueberries, and peaches. Pecans, corn and wheat crops were also damaged. Economic losses totalling in the millions of dollars are anticipated. Lows in many valley locations dropped into the upper 20s both mornings. Many locations, especially rural, experienced temperatures below 32 degrees Fahrenheit from 20 to 30 hours over the span of the 6th through 10th. ||Normally, the probability of temperatures dipping into the 20s this late in the season is 10 percent or less. Also of note, NASA MODIS remote sensing satellite imagery over the central Tennessee Valley revealed distinct changes from green vegetative and tree color before to an auburn and brown color, especially notable atop the Cumberland Plateau region of northeast Alabama and southern middle Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August was the hottest month on record for portions of southern middle Tennessee. Numerous daily temperature records were set, including several days reaching 100 degree plus high temperatures. In fact, Winchester set an all time record high temperature with 103 degrees on the 16th. This extreme heat only magnified the ongoing drought.||As far as rainfall goes, August can be described as a tale of two extremes.  Only spotty showers occurred the first week of the month, with many areas staying dry.  After that, virtually no rain occurred through August 23rd.  Then a significant precipitation event occurred on August 24th||Despite these rain events, exceptional (D4) drought conditions remained all month across Franklin and southeastern Lincoln County, with extreme (D3) drought conditions elsewhere.  This area was also downgraded to exceptional (D4) conditions before month's end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions declined once again despite some areas receiving rainfall.  Although more widespread rainfall occurred in July as a whole, most of it came in scattered to numerous afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms.  By the end of the month, exceptional (D4) drought conditions had been reassigned to eastern portions of southern middle Tennessee along and near the Cumberland Plateau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May became the month that plunged the area into a historic drought situation.  The D3 status (Extreme Drought) on the U.S. Drought Monitor was retained for the entire month.  |Area rivers remained at baseflow, and some reached historically low|streamflows, the lowest recorded for this time of year in more than|50 years. Farm ponds experienced lowered levels as well. Some|smaller creeks which typically would have water in them after a wet|Spring are reported to be dry.  Lake levels were below normal, including Tims Ford Lake.  Mandatory water restrictions were placed into effect in Winchester, Tennessee.|Soil moisture was at historic lows, at the first percentile or|below.  Hay cutting ran behind and at a lower production rate. Non-irrigated corn in some areas was believed to be a complete loss. Overall yields were reduced and the dry conditions caused even further reductions in the expected yields. Local extension agents rated the|corn and wheat crops as poor to very poor.|Extension agents also reported that cotton and soybeans were stressed|due to lack of soil moisture. Pasturelands produced very low yields of|hay due to lack of growth, thus farmers have been forced to reduce|cattle herds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: May became the month that plunged the area into a historic drought situation. The D3 status (Extreme Drought) on the U.S. Drought Monitor was retained for the entire month. ||Area rivers remained at baseflow, and some reached historically low |streamflows, the lowest recorded for this time of year in more than |50 years. Farm ponds experienced lowered levels as well. Some |smaller creeks which typically would have water in them after a wet |Spring are reported to be dry. Lake levels were below normal, including Tims Ford Lake. Mandatory water restrictions were placed into effect in Winchester, Tennessee. ||Soil moisture was at historic lows, at the first percentile or |below. Hay cutting ran behind and at a lower production rate. Non-irrigated corn in some areas was believed to be a complete loss. Overall yields were reduced and the dry conditions caused even further reductions in the expected yields. Local extension agents rated the |corn and wheat crops as poor to very poor. Extension agents also reported that cotton and soybeans were stressed due to lack of soil moisture. Pasturelands produced very low yields of hay due to lack of growth, thus farmers have been forced to reduce cattle herds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: March, traditionally the wettest month of the year, was instead one of the driest on record in 2007.  Rainfall totals were less than two inches across the entire Central Tennessee Valley, with some areas seeing less than an inch.  Normal rainfall for March is six to seven inches.|By the end of the month, a significant portion of the area had been downgraded to D3 (Extreme Drought) status on the U.S. Drought Monitor.  As a result, NWS Huntsville began issuing the drought statement at least twice per week.|A few small grass fires developed during this period, but no known damages or injury reports were received.

A few locations tallied near normal rainfall for the month, including Winchester.  |However, the region spent the first half of April in the D3 status (Extreme Drought) on the U.S. Drought Monitor, before improving to D2 (Severe Drought) for the latter half of the month. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The dry weather continued in April���but there was some limited short-term relief. A few locations tallied near normal rainfall for the month, including Winchester. However, the region spent the first half of April in the D3 status (Extreme Drought) on the U.S. Drought Monitor, before improving to D2 (Severe Drought) for the latter half of the month. Impacts were reported from across the area by agricultural interests. A late season freeze on April 7th and 8th added to the agricultural stress as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Governor Phil Bredesen had requested federal disaster drought assistance aid for farmers across all 95 counties in Tennessee on July 5, 2007. The request was approved by the USDA on July 31. The assistance will give an opportunity for farmers to receive low interest loans. ||The impact of the drought continued into July. The Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) average system inflow above Chickamauga Dam for the week ending July 8 was 4,010 cubic feet per second (cfs). That inflow for the week ending July 8 ranks 105th out of 105 years of record for this time of year. Tennessee has 37 drinking water supply systems taking voluntary or mandatory conservation measures. That is up from 36.||The lack of rainfall is making it difficult for TVA to keep river reservoirs full. As a result, hydroelectric power production is being cut by 50 percent. ||TVA is having to purchase electricity from more expensive sources, namely from electric plants that produce electricity from fossil fuels.|This increased cost to TVA will be passed along to consumers as early as October 1, 2007.||The Jackson County Utility District issued a State of Emergency and water restrictions rules for its customers. Second time violators of these restrictions will have their water meter taken out until restrictions are lifted and fines are paid.||The State of Emergency was declared by the Red Boiling Springs Mayor and Water Commissioner. The State of Emergency was issued on July 17th.

An stagnant upper ridge brought a prolonged period of excessive heat to east Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the period, Chattanooga experienced temperatures at or above 94 degrees for 25 straight days, including 9 days over 100 degrees.  Five record highs temperatures were also broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon hours across east Tennessee.  Storm reports comprised of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Herald News in Dayton reported a section on roof from a barn and apartment near Dayton were damaged by winds.  Numerous trees were also reported downed by thunderstorm winds in the Dayton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance tracked through the region triggering isolated severe thunderstorms across northeast Tennessee during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  The storm reports comprised of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Greenville Sun newspaper reported high winds from a thunderstorm ripped the roof from an open storage building near Mohawk.  In addition a 14 foot camper trailer was destroyed in the same area.  The sheriffs dispatch reported numerous trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm winds from Mohawk to Mosheim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorm developed during the afternoon hours as the surface temperatures rose into the 90s and instability increased across east Tennessee.  Storm reports consisted of damaging winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a Kentucky Fried Chicken Restaurant's sign was blown off by thunderstorm winds in Greenville.  In addition, a credit union had some roof damage.  Bleachers at the fairground were also damaged by winds.  Trees were also downed in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near normal rainfall during the month of September, relaxed the drought from extreme(D3) conditions over much of West Tennessee to severe conditions(D2). In addition, locations near the Tennessee River, the drought conditions relaxed from an exceptional(D4) to exceptional(D3).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extreme drought(D3) relaxed to a severe drought(D2) over much of West Tennessee during September. For areas near the Tennessee River, the drought relaxed from exceptional(D4) to extreme(D3).  Rainfall amounts for September ranged from about 2 inches below normal to around an inch above normal, which brought the yearly deficit to around 17 to 19 inches below normal. The drought impacted agricultural and hydrological interests. Burn bans also remained in effect across West Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front pushed into unstable, tropical air overnight on the 24th, producing wind damage and flash flooding in many areas. As this cold front continued to push further southeast during the day on the 25th, thunderstorms ahead of the front produced isolated large hail as well damaging winds during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe heat wave brought high temperatures near or above the 100 degree mark during most of the month of August. The unusually warm temperatures combined with high dew points produced heat index values of 105 degrees or greater during the heat wave. Numerous heat related injuries and fatalities were reported across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot and Humid weather settled over West Tennessee during most of August. High temperatures reached near or above the 100 degree mark during the month with heat index values reaching above the 105 degrees. One heat-related injury was reported at the construction site of the new Hardin County Jail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal rainfall during the month of October, continued to relax the drought from severe(D2) conditions over much of West Tennessee to moderate conditions(D1).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: May became the month that plunged the area into a historic drought situation. The D3 status (Extreme Drought) on the U.S. Drought Monitor was retained for the entire month. ||Area rivers remained at baseflow, and some reached historically low |streamflows, the lowest recorded for this time of year in more than |50 years. Farm ponds experienced lowered levels as well. Some |smaller creeks which typically would have water in them after a wet |Spring are reported to be dry. Lake levels were below normal, including Tims Ford Lake. Mandatory water restrictions were placed into effect in Winchester, Tennessee. ||Soil moisture was at historic lows, at the first percentile or |below. Hay cutting ran behind and at a lower production rate. Non-irrigated corn in some areas was believed to be a complete loss. Overall yields were reduced and the dry conditions caused even further reductions in the expected yields. Local extension agents rated the |corn and wheat crops as poor to very poor. Extension agents also reported that cotton and soybeans were stressed due to lack of soil moisture. Pasturelands produced very low yields of hay due to lack of growth, thus farmers have been forced to reduce cattle herds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall deficits continued to grow in June, with only spotty and mainly light rain experienced throughout the month.  A pattern change toward the end of the month brought spotty short-term relief to a few, but on a long-term basis, the historic drought continued. The Drought Monitor downgraded much of southern middle Tennesee from the D3 (Extreme) to D4 (Exceptional) drought category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic blast of unseasonably record cold produced an unprecedented hard freeze to the entire Tennessee Valley on the mornings of April 7 and 8, and either freezing or near freezing conditions on the preceding (6th) and following day (9th). This resulted in total losses of area fruit crops including strawberries, blueberries, and peaches.  Pecans, corn and wheat crops were also damaged.  Economic losses totalling in the millions of dollars are anticipated. Lows in many valley locations dropped into the upper 20s both mornings.  Many locations, especially rural, experienced temperatures below 32 degrees Fahrenheit from 20 to 30 hours over the span of the 6th through 10th. |Normally, the probability of temperatures dipping into the 20s this late in the season is 10 percent or less. Also of note, NASA MODIS remote sensing satellite imagery over the central Tennessee Valley revealed distinct changes from green vegetative and tree color before to an auburn and brown color, especially notable atop the Cumberland Plateau region of northeast Alabama and southern middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic blast of unseasonably record cold produced an unprecedented hard freeze to the entire Tennessee Valley on the mornings of April 7 and 8, and either freezing or near freezing conditions on the preceding (6th) and following day (9th). This resulted in total losses of area fruit crops including strawberries, blueberries, and peaches. Pecans, corn and wheat crops were also damaged. Economic losses totalling in the millions of dollars are anticipated. Lows in many valley locations dropped into the upper 20s both mornings. Many locations, especially rural, experienced temperatures below 32 degrees Fahrenheit from 20 to 30 hours over the span of the 6th through 10th. ||Normally, the probability of temperatures dipping into the 20s this late in the season is 10 percent or less. Also of note, NASA MODIS remote sensing satellite imagery over the central Tennessee Valley revealed distinct changes from green vegetative and tree color before to an auburn and brown color, especially notable atop the Cumberland Plateau region of northeast Alabama and southern middle Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low triggered a line of severe thunderstorms along the Cumberland Plateau during the early morning hours.  Wind damage was the primary storm reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriffs dispatch reported one tree downed by thunderstorms winds on Pope Tee Road near Pikeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall deficits continued to grow in June, with only spotty and mainly light rain experienced throughout the month.  A pattern change toward the end of the month brought spotty short-term relief to a few, but on a long-term basis, the historic drought continued. The Drought Monitor downgraded much of southern middle Tennesee from the D3 (Extreme) to D4 (Exceptional) drought category.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall deficits continued to grow in June, with only spotty and mainly light rain experienced throughout the month. A pattern change toward the end of the month brought spotty short-term relief to a few, but on a long-term basis, the historic drought continued. The Drought Monitor downgraded much of southern middle Tennesee from the D3 (Extreme) to D4 (Exceptional) drought category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dry weather continued in April���but there was some limited short-term relief.  A few locations tallied near normal rainfall for the month, including Winchester.  |However, the region spent the first half of April in the D3 status (Extreme Drought) on the U.S. Drought Monitor, before improving to D2 (Severe Drought) for the latter half of the month.  Impacts were reported from across the area by agricultural interests.  A late season freeze on April 7th and 8th added to the agricultural stress as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The dry weather continued in April���but there was some limited short-term relief. A few locations tallied near normal rainfall for the month, including Winchester. However, the region spent the first half of April in the D3 status (Extreme Drought) on the U.S. Drought Monitor, before improving to D2 (Severe Drought) for the latter half of the month. Impacts were reported from across the area by agricultural interests. A late season freeze on April 7th and 8th added to the agricultural stress as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into West Tennessee during the evening hours of October 18th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front. Some of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some trees were knocked down in the Bolivar area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating produced scattered showers and thunderstorms over extreme Southwest Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of October 8th, 2007. Some of the thunderstorms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding on New Allen and Range Line Roads in the Frayser area. Streets were impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather broke out ahead of a cold front pushing into West and Middle Tennessee on the afternoon and evening of Nov. 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were uprooted in the Crieve Hall area of Nashville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into Arkansas during the evening hours of October 17th, 2007 into the early morning hours of October 18th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of the front. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and flash flooding across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Jackson. Up to 2 feet of water covered the intersection of Henderson and Ridgecrest Roads. In addition, Ashcroft Road and other streets were flooded in and around the Jackson area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved through the mid south on its way into Southwest Virginia and Eastern Tennessee.  A large bow developed on the line late in the evening as it approached the Cumberland Plateau. Damage was predominantly created by straight line winds. However, an EF1 Tornado also developed on the Cumberland Plateau in Claiborne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved through the mid south on its way into Southwest Virginia and Eastern Tennessee.  A large bow developed on the line late in the evening as it approached the Cumberland Plateau. Damage was predominantly created by straight line winds. However, an EF1 Tornado also developed on the Cumberland Plateau in Claiborne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms fired up ahead of a strong cold front on April 3, 2007. There were numerous reports of hail and wind damage. One tornado occurred in Cumberland County at 0916 PM CDT. Snow flurries were reported on Good Friday, April 6, and record cold morning low temperatures were common on Easter Sunday morning with readings in the teens and 20s across Middle Tennessee. Nashville Airport had a record low of 23 degrees Easter Sunday morning. A late season hard freeze hit the mid state. Lows temperatures across Middle Tennessee Friday morning were: 18-29 degrees, Saturday morning lows were 13-27 degrees, and Sunday morning lows were from 17-27 degrees. There were numerous damage to the  berries, fruit trees, and wheat crops across Middle Tennessee amounting to about $100,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Center reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance passed through the region triggering scattered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Large hail was the primary storm reports with one up to the size of softballs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported penny-size hail at Laurel Bloomery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance passed through the region triggering scattered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Large hail was the primary storm reports with one up to the size of softballs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported penny-size hail in the western portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather broke out ahead of a cold front pushing into West and Middle Tennessee on the afternoon and evening of Nov. 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to a mobile home and to the Almyra Methodist Church. The mobile home blew off its foundation, rolled across the street, slamming into a brick home across the street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into West Tennessee during the evening hours of October 18th, 2007. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front. Some of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some trees and power lines were knocked down on New Boston Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms was moving west to east across Macon County on the evening of July 17. A lightning bolt from a thunderstorm|destroyed a feed barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms was moving west to east across Macon County on the evening of July 17. A lightning bolt from a thunderstorm|destroyed a feed barn. The barn was located at 3716 Akersville Rd. The Lafayette F.D. was on the scene for about 90 minutes trying to put out the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough across the region triggered isolated strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours across the area.  Storm reports comprised of small hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Central dispatch reported two trees down on Toestring Valley Road near Spring City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through east Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours.  Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of it.  Damage reports consisted mainly of straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed by thunderstorm winds near the Carter/Sullivan county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours and produced several reports of wind damage. This activity occurred as an upper level disturbance tracked east through the lower Mississippi Valley encountering a very moist and unstable airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds produced heavy damage in a half mile wide path.  The winds stripped a roof off of a large barn and blew down numerous trees which fell on garages, cars and outbuildings.  The winds also downed power and phone lines in the area.

A strong cold front moved into West Tennessee during the late afternoon and evening hours of April 3, 2007. The front produced numerous showers and thunderstorms many of which became severe. Damaging winds and large hail occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to golfball size hail fell around Millington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front pushed into unstable, tropical air overnight on the 24th, producing wind damage and flash flooding in many areas. As this cold front continued to push further southeast during the day on the 25th, thunderstorms ahead of the front produced isolated large hail as well damaging winds during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Backyard Observer measured 4.05 inches of rain at Midnight and an additional inch of rain estimated between Midnight and 12:15 am.
